design_philosophy.core_loop: Progress through levels by defeating enemy waves and bosses, collecting power-up symbols (Crane Kicks, Drum Punches) that enhance attacks and restore health
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Escalating enemy difficulty across four levels with environmental hazards (typhoon winds, falling objects) and protective mechanics (rescuing characters)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody Daniel LaRusso from the Karate Kid films, progressing from tournament fighter through Okinawan adventures to final confrontation, learning signature moves
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Integrates narrative-driven stage progression with film plot points; introduces protective mechanics where failure involves character safety (Kumiko drowning) rather than pure combat victory; bonus stage unlocks through non-combat skill challenges (catching flies, breaking ice blocks)

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives; losing Kumiko on Level 4 costs a life. Implied limited continues.
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; no mid-level checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal; difficulty scaling is fixed by level progression. Environmental hazards (typhoon) add uncontrollable challenge elements. Protective mechanics (saving Kumiko) introduce mandatory failure conditions outside direct combat.

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups via collectible symbols: 'C' symbols grant Crane Kicks, 'D' symbols grant Drum Punches. Both restore partial health. Appear after defeating enemy groups.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Hidden power-up unlocks through environmental interaction: breaking ice blocks, catching flies with chopsticks, dodging swinging hammer. Each level gates access to specific move sets.
replayability_hooks: Hidden entrances in Levels 2-3 reward exploration with power-up access
Scoring system implied through defeating throwaway enemies and environmental objects (typhoon hazards)
Four distinct level themes with unique enemy configurations and obstacles

world_building_techniques: Level progression mirrors film narrative: Tournament → Okinawa → Typhoon Rescue → Festival finale
Character presence in objectives: Johnny Lawrence as Level 1 final opponent, Chozen as recurring antagonist (Levels 2-4), Kumiko rescue objective on Level 4
Environmental storytelling through location changes and contextual hazards (typhoon wind mechanic on Level 3)
Ending cinematic with Mr. Miyagi voice narration acknowledging player mastery

boss_design.philosophy: Recurring antagonist (Chozen) with escalating mechanical complexity. Early bosses feature straightforward combat; final boss adds protective secondary objective (preventing Kumiko's death). Johnny Lawrence serves as tournament climax opponent.
notable_bosses: Johnny Lawrence (Level 1 Tournament Final) - presumed straightforward fighter; energy bar scales with player progression
Chozen Toguchi (Level 2 Okinawa) - standard combat encounter
Chozen Toguchi (Level 3 Typhoon) - rematch with protective objective: must rescue girl on pole; combat not mandatory, only rescue required

Chozen Toguchi (Level 4 Festival) - evolved variant with Kumiko protection mechanic; player must periodically contact Kumiko to prevent her drowning; requires simultaneous combat and protective management

faction_indicators: Cobra Kai implied through Johnny Lawrence and Chozen as antagonists
Miyagi-do represented through Daniel progression and ending validation
Thugs and karate students as undifferentiated enemy forces reflect film's 'outsider vs. establishment' dynamic
danger_signals: Okinawan thugs (Level 2) establish foreign danger and isolation
Chozen as persistent threat across multiple levels signals personal vendetta escalation
Environmental threats (typhoon) externalize danger beyond human antagonists
Tougher two-hit enemies on Level 4 and spear-wielding opponents indicate raising stakes
environmental_storytelling: All Valley Tournament setting grounds opening in film canon
Okinawa geography shift establishes cultural displacement and training journey
Typhoon weather hazard contextualizes Level 3 as climactic peril moment
Festival setting provides ceremonial conclusion space, suggesting cultural acceptance
